title: He’s running the 2026 Boston Marathon to fight addiction stigma
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: Kevin Hayden, 47, from North Easton, is running the 2026 Boston Marathon to support Learn to Cope.
Learn to Cope is a nonprofit offering support and education to families affected by addiction.
Hayden's run aims to raise awareness about addiction as a public health issue, combating stigma and misconceptions.

title: Social media celeb Alix Earle returned to Harvard this week
author: John Waller
bullets: Social media celebrity Alix Earle visited Harvard University to speak with Business School students this week.
Earle is collaborating on a case study with Professor Reza Satchu, who specializes in entrepreneurship at Harvard.
Earle's visit followed her previous appearance at Harvard last spring, where she engaged with students.

title: ‘There’s nothing compared to that feeling’: Red Sox players discussed return from World Baseball Classic
author: Hayden Bird
bullets: Red Sox players including Wilyer Abreu and Willson Contreras return to spring training after the World Baseball Classic.
Venezuela, featuring Red Sox players, won its first WBC crown, defeating Japan, the United States, and Italy.
Ranger Suarez noted the contrast between WBC crowds and the quieter atmosphere of Red Sox spring training.

title: Marlon Wayans stopped by this South Boston restaurant for lunch
author: John Waller
bullets: Marlon Wayans visited Hunter's Kitchen & Bar in South Boston while touring Massachusetts.
Wayans shared an Instagram story praising chef Lambert Givens's southern cuisine.
Wayans's "Wild Child Tour" had shows in Worcester and New Bedford this week.

title: Nine Winters Bakery is closing in Cambridge: ‘I’m choosing my family’
author: Katelyn Umholtz
bullets: Nine Winters Bakery in Cambridge is closing on March 29 after being open for less than a year.
Owner Marissa Ferola is closing the bakery to prioritize family and time with her daughters.
The bakery was known for Korean-American treats like Gochujang sticky buns and kimchi cheese danishes.

title: Stephen Curry swung by this North End restaurant while in town for Celtics-Warriors
author: John Waller
bullets: Stephen Curry visited Strega in Boston's North End while the Warriors were in town for a game.
Curry, not playing due to a knee injury, was seen at the restaurant with host David Ledbury.
Strega shared an Instagram post celebrating Curry's visit, describing the restaurant as a blend of championship energy and Italian indulgence.

title: It’s finally payday for former Boston Sports Clubs members
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: Massachusetts Attorney General's Office is distributing $4 million to about 47,000 former Boston Sports Clubs members.
Court ruled former CEO Patrick Walsh unlawfully charged members during COVID-era closures from mid-March to July 2020.
Boston Sports Club rebranded as New York Sports Club in 2023, now operating three locations in Boston area.

title: Beat out by a hot tamale: Maine hosts the second-best specialty food festival in America, according to USA Today readers
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: The Maine Whoopie Pie Festival in Dover-Foxcroft ranked No. 2 in USA Today's 2026 10Best Readers’ Choice Awards.
The festival features competitive bakers, eating contests, and entertainment, attracting whoopie pie lovers to Piscataquis Valley Fairgrounds on June 13.
Mississippi's Delta Hot Tamale Festival claimed the top spot as the best specialty food festival in America.

title: Man accused of recording people in South Shore library bathroom
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: A hidden recording device was found in a restroom at Paul Pratt Public Library in Cohasset.
Thomas Campbell, 63, of Marshfield, was arrested and charged with illegal wiretapping and video recording.
Campbell is held on $2,500 bail and is scheduled for arraignment Friday in Quincy District Court.

title: Energy assistance runs out amid spiking oil prices
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: Heating oil prices in Massachusetts surged 30% due to the war in Iran, straining fuel-assistance budgets.
Action for Boston Community Development reported 70% of clients exhausted their fuel-assistance benefits amid harsh winter.
Gov. Healey raised maximum fuel-assistance benefits to $1,400 to counter rising costs, but demand still exceeds available aid.
